_about: Curated scene summaries for non-Atalanta plates in AlchemyDB. Keyed by image_id (string). Used at build time to enrich AlchemyDB image text for auto-tagging in the visual browser. Read-only with respect to AlchemyDB itself.

63.scene: Outside the city walls a great washing-festival unfolds: women launder white sheets in tubs and stretch them on the green to bleach in the sun. The whitening of cloth is the visible analogue of the alchemical albedo, the work of purifying the dark prima materia into a luminous white earth.

64.scene: Citizens of a Renaissance town are absorbed in a great washing-festival: laundresses, basins, and white sheets stretched in the sun fill the foreground while the walled city rises behind. The plate is read as the albedo, the whitening operation in which the philosophical matter is washed clean of nigredo.

65.scene: An alchemist in scholar's robes consults his books in a vaulted laboratory while an apprentice tends the athanor. Glass vessels, retorts, and an alembic are arrayed on the workbench; a furnace glows in the background and smoke rises from the chimney.

66.scene: An alchemist works at a tall furnace, his apprentice handing him a long-necked alembic. Books and instruments crowd the laboratory; the philosophical egg gestates over slow fire.

68.folio: Berlin codex, Plate 10 — Severing the King
68.scene: A black-armoured executioner severs the head of a crowned king in a meadow; blood streams down to fertilize the ground. The decapitation depicts mortificatio: the killing of the metallic king, dissolution of his old form so that the dismembered body may yield the philosophical mercury.

69.folio: Berlin codex, Plate 11 — Body in the Vessel
69.scene: A naked body floats in a great glass philosophical vessel set over the athanor; flames lick the base, and dark fumes rise within. The killed king is now boiled in the egg — putrefaction and dissolution proceeding under sustained gentle fire.

70.scene: Saturn, an old man with scythe and crutch, stands above the alchemical vessel within which a winged dragon devours its young. Lead, melancholy, and the nigredo are concentrated in the saturnine register; the dragon-and-child motif marks the killing-and-renewing core of the work.

71.scene: Jovial Jupiter on his throne presides over a flask in which three birds rise: the philosophical bird ascending, descending, and returning. The avian sublimation marks the transition from saturnine darkness toward solar gold under Jupiter's tin-blue planetary patronage.

75.scene: The Golden Fleece (Aureum Vellus) is shown on a tree in the dragon-guarded grove of Colchis: Jason approaches with sword raised while the serpent coils around the trunk. The fleece is the perfected gold, the dragon the volatile mercury that must be subdued.

76.scene: Two crowned figures stand against a starry ground: a queen in silver-white robes (Mercury, Luna, the philosophical mercury) and a king in red and gold (Sulphur, Sol, the philosophical sulphur). Their meeting is the chemical wedding, the conjunctio of the two principles.

77.scene: Detail of the crowned queen Mercury and king Sulphur clasping hands in the sacred marriage; behind them the alchemical vessel awaits the union. The hierogamy of Sol and Luna is rendered as royal nuptials.

79.scene: The crowned hermaphrodite (Rebis) stands triumphant on a hilltop holding sun and moon, peacock and chalice, with red and white roses at the feet. The two-natured androgyne is the perfected Stone, the rubedo achieved at last.

52.scene: The frontispiece of the silent book: an alchemist sleeps beside a stream while two angels descend a Jacob's ladder to address him. Hermes Trismegistus and the philosophical patron preside; the muteness of the work is itself the lesson.

53.scene: An alchemical couple kneels at the athanor in a walled garden; above them angels hold the philosophical sun and moon. Dew-collection cloths are stretched on the grass, the celestial dew descending into the morning vessels.

54.scene: Two alchemists, man and woman, work side by side at a furnace: she turns a philosophical egg while he tends the fire. The Mutus Liber frames the opus as a lay-couple's domestic discipline rather than a court physician's craft.

55.scene: Inside a domestic laboratory the alchemist couple distil dew through linen cloths into a flask, gathering ros coelestis at dawn. The descending celestial moisture is the privileged solvent of the silent book.

58.folio: Mutus Liber — Putti and the Stone
58.scene: Two winged putti hold aloft a great philosophical stone in which Hermes Trismegistus is enthroned; below, a man and woman kneel before the furnace where the chemical wedding will take place. The stone glows like a gem, its central image the patron of the art.

60.scene: Concentric circles of creation: the divine name at the centre, four elemental rings outward, and the zodiacal band of stars at the rim. The cosmological scheme grounds the silent book's operative practice in macrocosmic correspondence.

62.scene: The alchemical couple and their angelic helpers gather around the fully ripened philosophical egg; the man elevates the flask while the woman registers the moment of completion. The book ends in silence with the rubedo achieved.

130.scene: A great serpent bites its own tail in a closed circle on a grassy hill: the ouroboros, sign of the closed work in the sealed vessel. The dragon is at once devourer and devoured.

131.scene: A unicorn and a stag meet in a forest clearing: emblem of the union of philosophical Mercury (the unicorn) and Sulphur (the stag), Spirit and Soul, that prepare the chemical wedding. The animal pair is read as the contrary principles whose conjunction is the work's heart.

132.scene: An old father in royal robes embraces his crowned son in a paradisal garden; an angel descends with the sign of the spirit. The triadic figure of father, son, and spirit (anima) names the trinitarian completion of the opus.

80.scene: A winged sun crowned with a starry diadem hovers above a furnace and philosophical vessel: emblem of the volatile-and-fixed solar principle that the rosarium will distil through its twenty figures.

81.scene: The green lion crouches with the sun in its jaws, devouring the solar disc whole. Vitriolic mercury swallowing gold: the operative dissolution of the solar principle by which the work begins.

82.scene: A crowned king in red and a crowned queen in white meet in a flowering meadow, exchanging branches and clasping hands. The royal couple are Sol and Luna preparing for the chemical wedding.

83.scene: A great philosophical fountain of mercury runs in three streams, surmounted by sun and moon and crowned with a stellar serpent. The mercurial source is the prima materia from which the entire work issues.

84.folio: Rosarium plate 2 — King and Queen meet
84.scene: The crowned king and queen face each other in formal meeting; doves of the spirit descend between them. The royal pair will be married, conjoined, killed, and revived through the rosarium's sequence.

87.scene: The naked king and queen lie embracing within the philosophical vessel, blackening as they are consumed by the slow fire of fermentation. Their united body is the corpus that must die before it can give birth to the new self.

96.folio: Rosarium — Green Lion consuming the sun
96.scene: The green lion (vitriolic mercury) tears at the solar disc, its open jaws crimson with gold. Decay before glory: the most-reproduced single image of the rosarium tradition.

97.folio: Rosarium — Marriage of Sun and Moon
97.scene: The crowned Sol and crowned Luna lie naked in the bath of the philosophical vessel, coupling under a descending dove of the spirit. The chemical wedding consummated.

98.folio: Rosarium — Marriage of Sun and Moon (2nd version)
98.scene: Variant of the Sol-and-Luna nuptial bath: the crowned solar king and lunar queen lie embracing under the descending spirit-dove, the philosophical vessel surrounding their marriage.

100.scene: A red-and-green lion holds the sun in its jaws and consumes it whole. The corrosive dissolution of solar gold by mercurial substance — the rosarium's iconic shorthand for the work's first death.

101.scene: Within the philosophical vessel the dead king and queen lie united on a slab as the soul (a small naked figure) is reborn from a cloud above; the spiritus returns to fix in the body. The fixation completes what fermentation began.

102.scene: The two-headed crowned hermaphrodite stands on a winged dragon, holding sun and moon, with a tree of seven planetary fruits at the side. The Rebis is the perfected matter of the rosarium, two natures in one body.

150.scene: A dark-winged angel descends with sword raised in the nigredo: figure of the saturnine spirit that presides over putrefaction and the long darkness of the work's first phase.

152.folio: Aurora consurgens — Conjoined Sun and Moon
152.scene: Sun and Moon are joined as a single conjoined figure within a philosophical mandorla: the cosmic conjunctio in personified form, sol and luna fused at the apex of the work.

153.scene: A monkey holds a mirror — the ape of nature, mimic of the alchemist, who can imitate the operations but not penetrate their meaning. The animal critique of mere technique.

155.scene: A sower casts seed onto a furrowed field of white earth, his bag at his hip: the philosophical sowing in which the gold-seed is planted in the prepared albedo earth to ripen into the Stone.

156.scene: Two crowned lovers embrace in a garden: the chemical wedding of the king and queen, Sol and Luna conjoined in the philosophical bridal chamber.

157.scene: Personified Sun and Moon face each other across a column, raising solar and lunar discs respectively: the polarity that organizes the alchemical cosmos.

158.scene: The wheel of the zodiac surrounds an inner ring of seven planetary deities: the celestial scaffold that governs the timing and quality of every alchemical operation.

162.scene: The peacock spreads its iridescent tail (cauda pavonis) above a philosophical vessel: the many-coloured display marking the transition from albedo through citrinitas, the matter signaling its life.

163.scene: A great pot boils on the athanor while the alchemist tends the fire; vapours rise from the philosophical vessel. Sustained slow heat governs the whole work.

167.scene: A dragon coils into a ring biting its tail, set within a paradisal garden: the ouroboros of the work, the closed circulation in which dissolution and coagulation feed each other.

168.folio: Aurora consurgens — Dragon in the Pot
168.scene: A dragon writhes inside the philosophical vessel as flames rise around it: the volatile mercury under the assault of fire, dying within the closed work.

170.scene: A flaming bird (the philosophical phoenix or salamander) rises from a fire, wings outstretched: the incombustible essence revealed by ordeal, the soul that survives every burning.

171.scene: A bearded digger excavates the dark earth with a spade, exposing a hidden vein of metal: the descent into matter (visita interiora terrae) at the root of vitriol's alchemical motto.

174.scene: A winged mercurial figure with caduceus stands in the centre of an alchemical landscape, mediating between earth and heaven: the philosophical mercury as messenger between the spheres.

175.scene: A dark-winged angel oversees a stage of the nigredo: the angelic intelligence of saturnine darkness, presiding over the necessary deaths of the work.

176.folio: Aurora consurgens — Chimera in the Pot
176.scene: A many-headed chimera (lion, dragon, eagle) writhes in the philosophical vessel above the fire: the composite monster of unrefined matter dying so its faculties may be unified into the Rebis.

177.folio: Aurora consurgens — Dragon on the Ground
177.scene: A wounded dragon lies on bare ground, its blood seeping into the soil: the volatile mercury slain on the earth-element so its essence may be fixed into the body of the Stone.

207.scene: The full Ripley Scroll: a vertical procession of alchemical figures including a tonsured alchemist, a winged dragon, a king and queen, a hermes-figure, and a great philosophical vessel atop a tower from which sun, moon, and stars stream. The scroll narrates the entire opus from prima materia to perfected gold.

208.scene: A great golden-winged bird (philosophical bird / phoenix) rises within a flask atop the alchemical tower; below, the alchemist contemplates the rising volatile spirit. The avian ascent marks sublimation and the recovery of the soul.

209.scene: A bearded alchemist holds aloft a glowing philosophical flask in which the Stone has ripened: the patron-figure of the scroll, presenting the work's product to the viewer.

221.folio: Alchemists Revealing Secrets — Book of Seven Seals
221.scene: Crowned alchemists open the Book of Seven Seals with apocalyptic imagery: the breaking of seals corresponds to the seven stages of the work, each unsealing revealing a new operation.

222.scene: Variant of the Ripley Scroll: the alchemist below the tower; above, the philosophical bird rising in the flask; sun, moon, and a great red-and-green dragon flank the central column. The scroll-form sequences the whole opus vertically.

200.scene: Hermes Trismegistus, the thrice-greatest patriarch of the alchemical art, stands robed and crowned holding the emerald tablet and the philosophical staff. Egypt is the first of Maier's twelve nations; Hermes is the source from which the prisca sapientia descends to the moderns.

201.folio: Symbola Aureae Mensae — Hermes the Mage
201.scene: Variant portrait of Hermes Trismegistus as alchemical mage with staff and tablet, presiding over the symbolic table. The patron of the art is depicted in the regalia of an ancient sage-king.

202.scene: Title page of Maier's Symbola Aureae Mensae Duodecim Nationum (1617): an architectural cartouche frames the title; twelve heraldic shields representing the twelve nations of alchemical lineage surround the central image, with Hermes Trismegistus presiding above.

203.scene: The plate of Melchior Cibinensis (the Hungarian patriarch): a mitred alchemist-priest celebrates the alchemical eucharist at an altar, the philosophical mass through which transmutation is understood as analogous to the sacrament.

204.scene: Engraving from the Symbola Aureae Mensae: a crowned philosopher holds a flask of tincture before a furnace; his coat of arms identifies him as one of the twelve patriarchs, his attribute (book, animal, or substance) marking his tradition.

205.scene: Wellcome library plate from the Symbola Aureae Mensae: a robed alchemical patriarch holds his characteristic instrument — a flask, a book, or a planetary metal — embedded within a heraldic cartouche.

206.scene: Complete folio scan of the Symbola Aureae Mensae: text and emblematic plates depicting the twelve patriarchs of alchemy with their nations and attributes.

272.folio: Symbola Aureae Mensae — Maria the Jewess
272.scene: Maria the Jewess, second of the twelve patriarchs (Hebrew), stands beside the bain-marie (named for her) — the philosophical water-bath that gently heats the sealed vessel. Her flask and the balneum maris are her attributes.

273.scene: Democritus the Greek, third patriarch, holds his Physica et Mystica before the philosophical egg. Greek atomism is read by Maier as alchemical doctrine — the indivisible element corresponds to the alchemical seed.

274.folio: Symbola Aureae Mensae — Morienus the Roman
274.scene: Morienus, the Roman patriarch and teacher of King Khalid, holds the philosophical stone in a flask. The transmission of the art from Greco-Roman to Arab traditions is encoded in the apprentice-master narrative he founded.

275.scene: Avicenna (Ibn Sīnā), the Arab patriarch, stands robed in scholarly garb before his Canon, holding a flask of philosophical mercury. The Arabic transmission of alchemical knowledge to medieval Europe is staged through his figure.

276.scene: Albertus Magnus, the German patriarch, in Dominican habit with mitre or scholar's cap, holds the Compositum de compositis before a philosophical retort. The medieval German tradition descends through him.

277.scene: Roger Bacon, the English patriarch, in Franciscan habit, holds the Speculum Alchemiae and contemplates a philosophical egg. Oxford's experimentalist friar represents the English alchemical lineage.

278.folio: Symbola Aureae Mensae — Arnoldus de Villa Nova
278.scene: Arnaldus of Villanova, the Spanish patriarch, holds the Rosarium and a flask of perfected tincture. The medieval Catalan-Provençal tradition speaks through his figure.

279.scene: Raymond Lull (Ramon Llull), the Catalan patriarch, holds his Ars Magna and the great wheels of his combinatorial philosophical method. Lullian computation underwrites alchemical correspondence.

280.scene: Thomas Aquinas, the Italian patriarch (here taken as alchemical author of the Aurora Consurgens attributed to him), in Dominican habit with the philosophical sun on his chest. Theology and alchemy meet in his figure.

281.scene: Michael Sendivogius, the Polish-Bohemian patriarch and contemporary of Maier, holds the Novum Lumen Chymicum. The early-modern Sarmatian lineage is one of Maier's near-contemporary witnesses.

282.folio: Symbola Aureae Mensae — Norton or Ripley
282.scene: An English alchemical patriarch (Thomas Norton or George Ripley) holds his Ordinall of Alchemy or his Twelve Gates, presiding over the philosophical work.

283.scene: Michael Maier presents himself as the twelfth and final patriarch, the German contemporary completing the chain of twelve nations. He holds his own Atalanta Fugiens and gestures toward the philosophical egg.

247.scene: Frontispiece of pseudo-Geber's Summa Perfectionis Magisterii: the alchemist at his workbench surrounded by alembics, retorts, and a great athanor. The thirteenth-century Latin text founds the corpus that medieval and Renaissance practitioners called 'Geber.'

248.scene: Plate showing a complex distillation apparatus: cucurbit on the furnace, alembic head fitted on top, condensing column descending into a receiving flask. The Geberian texts are foundational for the iconography of distillation.

249.scene: Comparative plate of alembics: a tall water-bath, a sand-bath cucurbit, a reverberatory furnace with retort, and a small lamp-furnace. Geber catalogues the equipment of the practical art.

250.scene: A calcination furnace with its open hearth and ash-pit: the matter is reduced to powder by sustained dry heat. Calcination is the first of the Geberian operations.

251.scene: A sublimation vessel: a long-necked aludel mounted over the fire, in which volatile spirits rise and condense in the cool upper chamber. Sublimation purifies the mercurial principle.

252.scene: Various retorts on the workbench: glass and earthenware vessels for distillation, with their long curved necks designed to channel vapor into receiving flasks. The Geberian apparatus is meticulously drawn.

253.scene: A flask of dissolved metal in mercurial water sits on a sand-bath: the dissolution of the body in the philosophical solvent, a key Geberian operation.

254.scene: The coagulation vessel: a sealed flask in which dissolved matter is fixed by gentle heat back into solid form. The closing of the work answers the opening dissolution.

300.scene: Frontispiece of Johann Rudolf Glauber's Furni Novi Philosophici (1646–49), 'New Philosophical Furnaces': an alchemist before a battery of innovative furnaces, demonstrating Glauber's reformed iatrochemical practice.

301.scene: Glauber's first novel furnace: a brick-built distillation furnace with multiple chambers, allowing simultaneous control of temperature gradients. The retort sits on a dedicated firebox; condenser tubes descend into receiving flasks.

302.scene: Glauber's second novel furnace: a wind furnace whose tall chimney creates draft to intensify combustion; iron retorts on the hearth; designed for the production of mineral acids and the famous sal mirabile (sodium sulphate).

303.scene: Glauber's third novel furnace: a reverberatory furnace whose flame is reflected back onto the matter from the arched roof, raising the temperature without direct contact between fire and crucible.

304.scene: Glauber's fourth novel furnace: a descensory furnace in which volatile components descend (rather than rise) into a lower chamber, useful for collecting heavy distillates that would lose energy if forced upward.

305.scene: A lamp furnace: an oil lamp providing controllable gentle heat for delicate operations such as the digestion of organic matter or the slow circulation of philosophical mercury.

306.scene: Glauber demonstrates the assembly of a complete distillation apparatus: cucurbit, alembic head, condensing tube, receiving flask, and the supporting iron stand on the furnace top.

307.scene: A sand-bath (balneum arenae): the flask is buried in heated sand for indirect, even heat distribution; preferred for operations requiring temperatures above water's boiling point but below direct flame.

308.scene: A water-bath (balneum mariae) as Glauber redesigned it: the flask sits suspended in heated water, the gentlest of indirect heating methods, named for Maria the Jewess.

223.scene: Frontispiece of Andreas Libavius's Alchymia (1597), the first systematic textbook of alchemy: an alchemist surrounded by furnaces, vessels, and books, presenting the discipline as a teachable science.

224.scene: Libavius's famous diagram of the ideal alchemical laboratory: a cross-section showing the chemist's workshop with furnaces, distillation apparatus, drying chambers, and storage shelves — the prototype of the modern chemistry lab.

225.scene: A chain of connected distillation apparatuses: cucurbit, alembic, retort, and pelican vessel arranged so that the volatile spirits cycle between vessels, enabling repeated rectification.

226.scene: The pelicanus: a circulatory flask whose distillate flows back onto its own residue through internal tubes, named for the legendary self-sacrificing bird. Used for sustained digestion and circulation.

227.scene: A retort with its receiving flask: the long-necked vessel allows vapor to condense and drip into the cooler receiver. Libavius standardized the iconography of laboratory equipment.

228.scene: Comparative plate of furnace types: athanor for slow steady heat, calcination furnace, reverberatory furnace, and lamp furnace, each with their characteristic openings and internal geometries.

180.scene: Frontispiece of Vannoccio Biringuccio's De la Pirotechnia (1540), the first printed treatise on metallurgy: smith and assayer at work, with bellows, furnace, and crucibles.

181.scene: A smelting furnace with bellows-driven blast: ore is reduced to metallic regulus in a refractory crucible. Biringuccio describes the assayer's craft as the practical foundation on which alchemy depends.

182.scene: Workshop scene with great double bellows feeding a blast furnace; molten metal is tapped from the hearth into a sand mould. The metallurgist's heat-management is the practical art alchemy theorizes.

183.scene: Casting bronze in a sand mould: the molten metal poured from a clay crucible into the mould-cavity. Biringuccio's plates document the early modern foundry.

184.scene: An assayer's balance for testing the noble-metal content of ores; alongside, a small cupellation furnace for refining gold and silver.

185.scene: Distillation apparatus for producing strong acids (aqua fortis, aqua regia): retorts heated on a sand-bath furnace, vapor condensing in receiving flasks for use in metallurgy and alchemy alike.

114.scene: An ore-prospector (Erzsucher) walks a forested hillside with his divining rod (virgula furcata), seeking veins of metal beneath the earth. Georgius Agricola's De Re Metallica (1556) treats the entire mining-and-smelting cycle.

115.folio: Agricola De Re Metallica — title page
115.scene: Title page of Agricola's De Re Metallica: an architectural cartouche surrounds the title, with mining and smelting scenes in the lower panels.

117.folio: Agricola De Re Metallica — full plate
117.scene: Composite plate from De Re Metallica showing the mining cycle: prospector locating the vein, miners cutting the ore, smelter reducing it in a furnace, and the assayer testing the regulus.

118.scene: The Rennwerk (smelt-mill, 1556 engraving): a great water-wheel powers the bellows that drive the blast furnace; molten metal is tapped into channels in the foreground. Agricola's plates anchor the iconography of Renaissance metallurgy.

119.folio: Agricola De Re Metallica — mining shaft
119.scene: Cross-section of a mining shaft: timbered galleries descending into the earth, miners with picks and lamps working ore-veins, and a windlass at the surface hauling buckets of rock. The descent into matter (visita interiora terrae) given technical reality.

120.folio: Agricola De Re Metallica — water-wheel pump
120.scene: A water-wheel-driven chain-pump removing water from a mineshaft: the mechanical engineering required to keep deep workings dry. Agricola documents the period's most advanced mining technology.
